1: “Elihu answered and said.” The formulaic speech-introduction marks the transition from Elihu’s address to Job (ch33) to his address to the wider company of wise men (v2). The dialogic structure of Job has always included an implicit audience beyond the immediate interlocutors; Elihu makes this explicit by appealing to ḥᵃkāmîm (wise men) and yōḏᵉʿîm (those who know/men of knowledge). He is placing his argument before a court of the wise — a move that both elevates the argument and implicitly invites the wise men to judge whether Job or Elihu is right.
2: “Hear my words, O wise men; listen to me, you who have knowledge.” Ḥᵃkāmîm (wise men) and yōḏᵉʿîm (those who know) — Elihu addresses the same audience the Friends implicitly spoke to throughout. The doubling of the invitation (hear/listen) is the standard wisdom-call of Proverbs (Prov 1:8; 4:10; 8:32-34). By addressing the wise men rather than Job, Elihu shifts register: this speech is less a personal confrontation than a public theological demonstration. The question at stake is whether God can act unjustly — and what wisdom tradition says about it.
3: “For the ear tests words as the palate tastes food.” Ḥēk yiṭʿam-ʾōkel (the palate tastes food) — the taste-wisdom metaphor recalls 12:11 where Job himself used it (“Does not the ear test words as the palate tastes food?”). Elihu may be consciously echoing Job’s own epistemology: discernment comes through careful testing, not reflexive judgment. The audience of wise men is being invited to taste Elihu’s argument as one tastes a complex dish — to evaluate it through experienced discernment rather than surface reaction. He is appealing to the wisdom tradition’s own self-understanding.
4: “Let us choose together what is right; let us determine among ourselves what is good.” Nibḥᵃrāh-lānû mišpāṭ (let us choose for ourselves justice/what is right) — the language is deliberative and communal: nibḥᵃrāh (let us choose, hortatory) and nēḏᵉʿāh (let us know/determine). Elihu frames the discussion as a shared inquiry rather than a tribunal. The bōn ʾêleymô mah-ṭôḇ (let us recognize among ourselves what is good) brings the wisdom criterion (ṭôḇ, good) alongside the legal criterion (mišpāṭ, justice). This is Elihu at his most collegial — and rhetorically careful: he is not yet pronouncing but inviting shared judgment.
5: “For Job has said, ‘I am righteous, and God has denied me my right.’” Ṣāḏaqtî (I am righteous) and ʾēl hēsîr mišpāṭî (God has removed/denied my right) — Elihu’s summary of Job’s central complaint. Job has indeed said both (27:2: “As God lives, who has denied me justice”; 31:35: “let the Almighty answer me”). The juxtaposition of Job’s self-declared righteousness and God’s denial of his right is the core tension of the book. Elihu will argue that this juxtaposition involves a category error: God cannot deny justice because God is the source and definition of justice.
6: “Must I lie about my case? My wound is past healing, though I have done no wrong.” This is quoted speech — either Elihu’s continuation of Job’s words or his own exclamation. Hᵃʿal-mišpāṭî ʾᵃḵazzēḇ (must I lie about my judgment/case?) — the rhetorical question expresses the dilemma: if God is just and Job suffers, either Job lied about his innocence or God is unjust. Ḥiṣṣî (my wound/arrow) — the “arrow” image for suffering echoes 6:4 (the arrows of the Almighty). Job’s complaint is presented at its sharpest before Elihu refutes it.
7: “What man is like Job, who drinks mockery like water?” Yišteh-laʿag kayyāyin (drinks mockery like water) — laʿag (mockery, scorn) as a beverage is a striking metaphor: Job consumes criticism so easily and continuously that it has become his drink. The comparison sharpens in v8: drinking mockery is associated with keeping company with evildoers. Elihu is beginning his critique: Job’s rhetoric has gone so far in accusing God that it amounts to companionship with the wicked. The “water” comparison contrasts with Job’s thirst for vindication (19:7; 30:20).
8: “Who keeps company with evildoers and walks with wicked men.” ʿᵃrāḇāh (pledges/associates with) and pōʿᵃlê ʾāwen (workers of iniquity) — Elihu accuses Job of rhetorical solidarity with the wicked: by claiming God acts arbitrarily, Job argues what the wicked claim (Ps 10:4; 14:1). Lāleḵeṯ ʿim-ʾanšê rešaʿ (to walk with wicked men) — the “walking” language picks up Ps 1:1. Elihu is not claiming Job is personally wicked; he is claiming Job’s theological argument, if correct, would make the wicked’s worldview right. This is a logical rather than moral charge.
9: “For he has said, ‘A man gains nothing by delighting in God.’” Lōʾ-yiskōn geḇer birṣōṯô ʿim-ʾĕlôhîm (a man gains nothing by his delight/favor with God) — this is Elihu’s most extreme summary of Job’s position, and it is a distortion. Job never said this directly; the closest is 21:15 (“What is the Almighty that we should serve him? What do we gain by praying to him?” — which is quoted speech of the wicked). Elihu has conflated Job’s complaint about God’s injustice with the wicked’s rejection of God entirely. This is an unfair characterization, which the divine verdict of 42:7-8 will implicitly correct.
10: “Therefore, men of understanding, listen to me: far be it from God to do evil, far from the Almighty to commit wrong.” Ḥālîlāh lāʾēl mērāšaʿ (far be it from God to do wrong) and wᵉšaddai mēʿāwel (and from the Almighty to do iniquity) — the ḥālîlāh formula is a strong rhetorical protest (cf. Gen 18:25, Abraham’s “far be it from you”; 1 Sam 12:23; Rom 6:1-2). This is the core of Elihu’s theodicy: God’s character is the argument. Not “God’s ways are too complex to understand” but “God cannot be other than just because rāšaʿ (wickedness) is definitionally incompatible with who God is.” This is stronger than the Friends’ retributive theodicy and anticipates the divine speeches.
11: “For he repays each person according to their work and deals with everyone according to their conduct.” Kî pōʿal ʾāḏām yᵉšallemnî-lô (for the work of a man he will repay him) — the retributive principle that the Friends also affirm, but Elihu uses it differently: not as accusation against Job but as proof of God’s justice-character. If God repays each according to work, then God is just by definition. The verse does not explain why Job suffers specifically; it establishes the framework within which the specific question must be asked. Elihu’s argument is structurally consistent even if it does not answer Job’s specific complaint.
12: “Of a truth, God will not act wickedly; the Almighty will not pervert justice.” ʾAp-ʾomnam ʾēl lōʾ-yarešîaʿ (truly, God will not act wickedly) — the ʾap-ʾomnam (surely, truly) is emphatic: Elihu is staking his argument on the impossibility of divine injustice. Yᵉʿawwēt mišpāṭ (pervert/distort justice) uses the ʿwt root that appeared in 8:3 (Bildad: “Does God pervert justice?”) — now Elihu gives a direct answer: no. The impossibility of God’s perverting justice is not a logical axiom but a theological claim about God’s character as Creator and sustainer.
13: “Who gave him his charge over the earth? Who appointed him over the whole world?” Mî-pāqaḏ ʿālāyw ʾāreṣ (who appointed him over the earth?) — the rhetorical questions expect the answer “no one.” God is not an appointed magistrate who could be removed or judged by a higher authority; God’s sovereignty is inherent, not delegated. This argument from ontology undergirds the claim of v10: God cannot be unjust because there is no external standard of justice above God to which God is answerable. This is Elihu’s most philosophically sophisticated point, and it anticipates the whirlwind speeches’ argument from creatorial incomparability.
14: “If he should set his heart against us and gather to himself his Spirit and breath.” Rûḥô wᵉnišmāṯô (his Spirit and his breath) — the paired terms of divine animating energy. Both rûaḥ (spirit/wind) and nišmāh (breath) are the source of all creaturely life (Gen 2:7; Job 33:4). If God were to withdraw his rûaḥ — gather it back to himself — the consequence follows in v15. The conditional im-yāśîm (if he were to set) is a counterfactual: God does not in fact do this continuously. But the bare possibility illuminates the full extent of creaturely dependence on divine sustenance. Life is not inherent to creatures; it is on permanent loan from God.
15: “All flesh would perish together, and human beings would return to dust.” Yigwaʿ kol-bāśār yāḥaḏ (all flesh would expire/perish together) and wᵉʾāḏām ʿal-ʿāpār yāšûḇ (and man would return to dust) — the reversal of Gen 2:7 and 3:19 (“you are dust and to dust you shall return”). The whole created order is ontologically dependent on God’s continued outbreathing. This is the positive form of the argument: God is not tyrannically arbitrary; God is the necessary condition for all existence. The God who sustains all life cannot be acting against the basic good of creatures. Elihu uses the cosmological argument in service of theodicy.
16: “If you have understanding, hear this; listen to what I am saying.” Elihu’s return to the direct imperative after the cosmological argument of vv13-15. Im-bināh (if you have understanding/discernment) — the bināh appeal is wisdom terminology (Prov 1:2; 4:5,7). He is now specifically addressing someone in the company — likely Job, who has been the implicit referent throughout ch34 despite the address to “wise men” in v2. The instruction-to-listen frame holds the speech together as pedagogical rather than judicial.
17: “Shall one who hates justice govern? Will you condemn the One who is righteous and mighty?” Hap-śônēʾ mišpāṭ yaḥbōš (shall one who hates justice bind/govern?) — the rhetorical question points to the absurdity of a justice-hating ruler. ʾIm-ṣaddîq kaббîr tᵉrašîaʿ (will you condemn the One who is righteous and mighty?) — Job’s position, Elihu argues, requires condemning God; but the one who is ṣaddîq (righteous) and kabbîr (mighty) cannot logically be condemned. The argument from divine character (v10-12) is now expressed as a logical impossibility: condemning a perfectly righteous ruler on the basis of one afflicted person’s complaint.
18: “Is it right to say to a king, ‘You are worthless,’ and to nobles, ‘You are wicked’?” The argument from analogy: even human royalty should not be addressed in such terms without overwhelming evidence and proper procedure. Bᵉliyyāʿal (worthless, wicked, base) — the term carries its full weight: not just ineffective but morally corrupt. The logic is a fortiori: if this would be improper with a king, how much more so with God (cf. Eccl 10:20). Job’s accusations against God have used language appropriate to condemning a corrupt human magistrate; Elihu argues the category is wrong.
19: “How much less to him who shows no partiality to princes and does not favor the rich over the poor, for they are all the work of his hands?” Ašer lōʾ-nāśāʾ pᵉnê śārîm (who does not lift up the face of princes — show partiality) — divine impartiality is the mark of genuine justice. The rich and poor are maʿᵃśēh yāḏāyw (the work of his hands) — equally formed, equally dependent. God’s justice cannot be bought or influenced by status. This is simultaneously a theological argument and an implicit rebuke of the Friends: their theodicy worked to justify the wealthy righteous and condemn the suddenly poor. God does not operate on those social categories.
20: “In a moment they die; at midnight the people are convulsed and pass away, and the mighty are removed — without human effort.” Peta (in a moment, suddenly) — the sudden death that removes even the mighty. Bᵉḥᵃṣî hallaylāh (at midnight) — the most unexpected time, the nadir of human alertness. Wᵉyᵃʿᵃḇrû wᵉyûsōr ʾabbîr lōʾ bᵉyāḏ (they pass away and the mighty are removed, not by hand) — death comes to the powerful without any human agent executing it. God removes rulers directly, without intermediaries. This is the sovereignty argument in dramatic form: no human power can insulate itself from divine removal.
21: “For his eyes are on the ways of everyone, and he sees all their steps.” Kî-ʿênāyw ʿal-darkê-ʾîš (his eyes are on the ways of a man) and yirʾeh kol-ṣᵃʿāḏāyw (he sees all their steps) — divine omniscience as the ground of impartial justice. God cannot be deceived or manipulated because he sees everything. This is not surveillance-as-oppression (Job’s reading of 7:17-18 and 10:14) but omniscient justice: God sees what the human magistrate cannot see and therefore can judge what the human magistrate cannot judge. Elihu frames what Job experienced as hostile watching as the precondition for fair judgment.
22: “There is no darkness or deep shadow where evildoers can hide themselves.” ʾEn-ḥōšeḵ wᵉʾên ṣalmāweṯ (there is no darkness and no shadow of death) where the wicked can hide. Ṣalmāweṯ (shadow of death / deep darkness) — the term Job used for his own condition (10:21-22; 12:22; 16:16; 28:3). For Job, ṣalmāweṯ was the place of his suffering; for Elihu, it is the place where the wicked futilely seek refuge. God’s omniscient reach extends even to ṣalmāweṯ. This reframes Job’s darkness not as abandonment but as the place God also sees.
23: “For he does not impose on anyone more than is warranted to bring them before God in judgment.” Lōʾ yāśîm ʿôḏ ʿal-ʾîš (he does not impose further on anyone) — the point is that God’s judicial process does not exceed what the case requires. The phrase is difficult but the MKT’s rendering captures the restraint: God is not extravagant in his judgments; he uses the minimum necessary weight to bring the person before his judgment. This limits the retributive excess the Friends implicitly assumed: God does not pile on beyond what justice requires.
24: “He shatters the mighty without investigation and sets others in their place.” Yāraʿ kabbîrîm lōʾ-ḥēqer (he shatters the mighty without investigation/without limit) — the divine removal of powerful rulers happens without the need for the usual judicial procedures. Wᵉyaʿᵃmēḏ ʾăḥērîm taḥtām (he sets others in their place) — the replacement is sovereign and immediate. This is not arbitrary tyranny but sovereign administration: God needs no drawn-out process to remove a corrupt ruler because his omniscience (vv21-22) means he already has all the evidence.
25: “For he knows their deeds, and he overturns them in the night; they are crushed.” Kî yāḏaʿ maʿᵃllêhem (for he knows their deeds/works) — the basis of the divine overturning is knowledge. The nighttime reversal (ballāylāh) echoes the sudden deaths of v20: midnight is when God moves against those who have built empires of oppression. Wᵉyiddakkᵉʾû (they are crushed/pulverized) — the same verb used for the crushing of the wicked elsewhere in Job (4:19; 5:4). The powerful rulers are not exceptions to God’s justice; they are subject to it on an accelerated schedule due to their visibility and responsibility.
26: “He strikes them for their wickedness in an open place where others can see.” Bᵉmaqqôm rᵉšāʿîm sᵉpāqām (in the place of the wicked he strikes them) and bimᵉqôm rōʾîm (in a place where those who see) — the public character of divine judgment against oppressive rulers is emphasized. The judgment is not hidden; it is done where it can be witnessed, so that the lesson is available to all. This is the positive form of the argument from v25: God does not just remove rulers privately; he makes the removal visible as a teaching event for the community.
27: “Because they turned away from following him and gave no thought to any of his ways.” ʾᵃšer ʿal-kēn sāru mēʾaḥᵃrāyw (because they turned from following him) — the reason for divine judgment: apostasy in leadership. Kol-dᵉrāḵāyw lōʾ hiśkîlû (they did not consider any of his ways) — the failure is cognitive-volitional: they did not attend to how God orders reality. The rulers who exploit the poor (v28) do so because they have systematically ignored God’s ways. Elihu’s theodicy here answers a question Job did not ask: why do wicked rulers fall? But it also implies: Job’s suffering does not fit this category.
28: “So that they brought the cry of the poor before him, and he heard the outcry of the afflicted.” Ṣaʿᵃqaṯ-dallîm (the cry of the poor/weak) and šawᵃʿaṯ ʿᵃniyyîm (the outcry of the afflicted) — God hears the cry that bypasses all earthly courts. The mechanism of cosmic justice operates through the cry of the oppressed: God responds to the outcry that has no earthly advocate. This is the prophetic tradition in its purest form (Exod 22:23; Prov 21:13). Elihu’s theodicy here is at its most compelling: the poor cry, God hears, rulers fall. But this raises the question Job embodies — what about when the righteous cry and there is silence?
29: “When he is quiet, who can condemn? When he hides his face, who can see him?” Wᵉhûʾ yašqîṭ ûmî yārešîaʿ (when he is quiet, who can condemn?) — Elihu acknowledges the phenomenon of divine silence that Job has experienced. Yastîr pānāyw (he hides his face) is exactly Job’s complaint in 13:24. Elihu’s response: even in the hiding, God cannot be condemned, because the hiding may be purposive. The “hiding of the face” is not abandonment but a mode of divine action. This is the closest Elihu comes to addressing Job’s actual experience — but he addresses it structurally, not personally.
30: “Lest a godless person reign and ensnare the people.” Mimmᵉlōḵ ʾāḏām ḥānēp (lest a godless/profane man reign) and môqᵉšê ʿām (snares of the people) — the purpose of divine sovereignty: the prevention of godless rule that traps populations. God’s removal of rulers is not arbitrary; it is structurally protective of communities. The social theology here is important: Elihu understands that individual theodicy (why does Job suffer?) cannot be separated from social theodicy (why do peoples suffer under evil rulers?). Both are addressed by the same divine justice that hears the cry of the poor.
31: “Has anyone said to God, ‘I have endured my punishment; I will not sin any more’?” Kî-ʾel-ʾēl ʾāmar nāśāʾtî (for has he said to God: I have borne/lifted it) — the rhetorical question opens a new section (vv31-37): what is the proper human response to divine discipline? Elihu presents the ideal response: submission and reform. The confession “I have endured my punishment” (nāśāʾtî can mean “I have borne my chastisement”) followed by reform — “I will not sin again” — is the posture Elihu implicitly commends to Job. Job’s refusal to adopt this posture is part of what Elihu finds problematic.
32: “And: ‘Teach me what I cannot see; if I have done wrong, I will not do it again.’” Hôrēnî ʾim-ʾāwen pāʿaltî lōʾ ʾōsîp (teach me; if I have done wrong, I will not do it again) — the idealized response adds the petition for divine instruction alongside the commitment to reform. This is the appropriate response to the dream-disciplines and suffering-chastenings Elihu described in ch33: “show me what I cannot see.” It is notable that even Elihu’s idealized penitent is conditional — “if I have done wrong”. But the implied expectation is that investigation will reveal some fault. Elihu cannot fully escape the Friends’ presupposition that suffering implies fault.
33: “Should he repay you on your own terms because you reject his?” Hᵉmimmᵉḵā yᵉšallᵉmennāh kî-māʾastā (should he repay you on your terms because you rejected his?) — Elihu challenges Job’s implicit demand that God operate on Job’s terms. The word māʾastā (you rejected/refused) is Elihu’s sharpest accusation in ch34: Job has rejected God’s terms. Whether this is accurate characterization of Job’s position is debatable; Job has not rejected God but has demanded accountability from God. Elihu conflates accountability-demand with rejection of God’s sovereignty, a conflation the divine speeches will not endorse.
34: “People of understanding will agree with me, and every wise person who hears me will say.” Elihu again appeals to the consensus of the wise (ʾanšê-lēḇāḇ, men of heart/understanding, and ḥāḵām, the wise man). This is a rhetorical closing move: before delivering the verdict (v35), Elihu frames it as what any wise person would conclude. The appeal to consensus is itself a wisdom convention — wisdom is not private but communally validated. The problem is that the verdicts of the “wise” the book has shown us (the Friends) have been wrong. Elihu’s confidence in consensus is ironically undercut by the narrative context.
35: “‘Job speaks without knowledge; his words are without insight.’” Iyyôḇ lōʾ-bᵉḏaʿaṯ yᵉḏabbēr (Job speaks not with knowledge) and ûḏᵉḇārāyw lōʾ bᵉhaśkēl (his words are not with insight) — Elihu delivers the verdict he has prepared. Ḏaʿat (knowledge) and haśkēl (insight/discernment) are both wisdom-epistemological terms; their absence is the wisdom tradition’s most serious charge. The divine speeches will use almost identical language: “Who is this who darkens counsel by words without knowledge?” (38:2). But where Elihu’s verdict is part of a prosecution, the divine version will be transformative — leading to Job’s restoration rather than condemnation.
36: “Would that Job were tested to the limit, because his answers are like those of wicked men.” ʾĀḇî yibbāḥēn Iyyôḇ ʿaḏ-nēṣaḥ (I wish that Job were tried to the uttermost) — Elihu’s zeal tips here into something darker: a wish for maximal testing. ʿal-tᵉšûḇōṯ bᵉʾanšê-ʾāwen (because his answers are like those of wicked men) — the association of Job’s rhetoric with ʾāwen (iniquity/wickedness) is the harshest thing Elihu says. He is wrong about this: the divine verdict (42:7) will vindicate Job’s speech as more honest than the Friends’ piety. Elihu’s error here is precisely what the book is designed to expose: equating theological impropriety with moral wickedness.
37: “For he adds rebellion to his sin; he mocks us and heaps up words against God.” Yōsîp ʿal-ḥaṭṭāʾṯô pešaʿ (he adds to his sin rebellion) — Elihu ends ch34 with the most severe indictment: Job’s continued speech is escalating from sin to pešaʿ (deliberate rebellion). Bênênû yispôq (he mocks/claps among us) and yarbeh ʾᵃmārāyw lāʾēl (he multiplies words against God). The final charge — multiplying words against God — is the opposite of Job’s actual condition: Job is pouring out suffering-speech, not blasphemy. The irony the book preserves is that God will vindicate these very “multiplied words” as more honest than Elihu’s measured theology.
